2605.06154.title: Graphlets as Building Blocks for Structural Vocabulary in Knowledge Graph Foundation Models
2605.06154.paper_summary: Foundation models excel at language, where sentences become tokens, and vision, where images become pixels, because both reduce to discrete symbols on a shared, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs share the discreteness, but not the geometry. Their entities and relations are discrete symbols, yet their arrangement is relational and lacks a common, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs (KGs) share the discreteness, but not the geometry. They form irregular, non-Euclidean topologies whose local neighborhoods differ from graph to graph. Therefore, Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs) rely on identifying structural invariances to produce transferable representations. Without a universal token set, KGFMs are limited in their ability to transfer representations across unseen KGs. We close this gap by treating graphlets, small connected graphs, as structural tokens that recur in heterogeneous KGs. In this paper, We introduce a model-agnostic framework based on a vocabulary of graphlets that mines a KG between relations via pattern matching. In particular, we considered closed and open 2- and 3-path, and star graphlets, to obtain robust invariances. The framework is evaluated on 51 KGs from a wide range of domains, for zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction. Experiments show that adding simple graphlets to the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KGFMs.
2605.06154.paper_summary_zh: 基礎模型在語言方面表現優異，句子變成標記；在視覺方面表現同樣出色，圖像變成像素，因為這兩者都可以簡化為共享的、固定的網格上的離散符號。知識圖譜共享離散性，但不具幾何性。它們的實體和關係是離散符號，但其排列是關聯性的，缺乏共同的固定網格。知識圖譜（KGs）共享離散性，但不具幾何性。它們形成不規則的、非歐幾里得的拓撲，其局部鄰域因圖而異。因此，知識圖譜基礎模型（KGFMs）依賴於識別結構不變性來生成可轉移的表示。沒有通用的標記集，KGFMs在跨未見KGs轉移表示的能力上受到限制。我們通過將圖小塊、小型連通圖視為在異質KGs中重複出現的結構標記來填補這一空白。在本文中，我們介紹了一個基於圖小塊詞彙的模型無關框架，通過模式匹配挖掘KG之間的關係。特別地，我們考慮了封閉和開放的2-和3-路徑以及星型圖小塊，以獲得穩健的不變性。該框架在來自廣泛領域的51個KGs上進行評估，用於零樣本歸納和傳導鏈接預測。實驗顯示，將簡單的圖小塊添加到詞彙中可產生優於先前KGFMs的模型。
2605.06154.author: Kossi Amouzouvi et.al.
2605.06154.authors: Kossi Amouzouvi, Robert Wardenga, Jens Lehmann, Sahar Vahdati
